does: This paper evaluates sparse autoencoders (SAEs) for steering language model behavior through task vector model editing on mathematical reasoning tasks in Gemma-3-4B-IT. The authors discover that projecting task vectors onto SAE feature subspaces creates an information bottleneck, discarding approximately 97% of modification energy and failing to produce statistically significant improvements across seven math subjects. They diagnose the root cause as geometric misalignment between activation-space SAE directions and weight-space task vectors. As a solution, they propose reframing SAEs as diagnostic tools rather than surgical intervention filters, using SAE-guided layer selection to identify where to inject unfiltered raw task vectors while preserving the full modification signal.
connects: This work extends prior research on mechanistic interpretability and model editing that has sought to make surgical interventions in neural networks. It builds on recent advances using SAEs as interpretability tools for understanding and steering model behavior, while engaging with the broader literature on task vectors and parameter-efficient fine-tuning. The paper responds to the apparent promise of feature-level editing approaches by rigorously testing whether SAE-guided projection actually improves editing outcomes, revealing limitations in applying activation-space interpretability tools directly to weight-space modifications. It connects to foundational work on geometric properties of neural network representations and the challenges of aligning different spaces in deep learning.
enables: This work opens new directions for interpretability-guided model editing by establishing that SAEs are better suited for diagnosis than intervention filtering. Future work could explore other interpretability methods for layer selection, develop techniques to resolve the geometric misalignment between activation and weight spaces, or investigate whether hybrid approaches combining SAE diagnostics with alternative projection methods yield better results. The framework also enables more rigorous evaluation standards for interpretability tools applied to model editing, pushing the field toward honest assessment of which interpretability techniques actually improve downstream task performance. This could inform better practices for domain-specific model enhancement and establish principles for when and how to use interpretability in the steering pipeline.
